questions.question: A 20-year-old male reports two days of diffuse abdominal pain that today localized to the lower right quadrant. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: A. Cholecystitis
B. Appendicitis
C. Diverticulitis
D. Pancreatitis
questions.rationale: Appendicitis classically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant near McBurney's point, matching this presentation.

questions.question: Which abdominal quadrant is classically associated with cholecystitis?
options: A. Right lower quadrant
B. Right upper quadrant
C. Left lower quadrant
D. Epigastric radiating to back
questions.rationale: Cholecystitis (gallbladder inflammation) classically presents with right upper quadrant pain.

questions.question: Which abdominal pain pattern is classic for pancreatitis?
options: A. RLQ pain
B. RUQ pain
C. Epigastric pain radiating to the back
D. Suprapubic pain
questions.rationale: Pancreatitis classically presents with epigastric pain that radiates to the back.

questions.question: A dialysis patient missed his last two treatments and now reports generalized weakness and tingling. What should you specifically screen for?
options: A. Hypoglycemia only
B. Hyperkalemia
C. Anaphylaxis
D. Simple dehydration
questions.rationale: Missed dialysis raises hyperkalemia risk, presenting with weakness, paresthesias, and potentially dangerous cardiac dysrhythmias — this patient needs a cardiac monitor and urgent evaluation.

questions.question: What physical exam findings indicate dehydration?
options: A. Bounding pulses and moist mucous membranes
B. Skin tenting (poor turgor) and dry mucous membranes
C. Warm, flushed, well-perfused skin
D. Normal capillary refill only
questions.rationale: Skin tenting and dry mucous membranes are classic bedside indicators of dehydration, useful even before formal vital sign changes appear.

questions.question: Which abdominal quadrant is classically associated with diverticulitis, especially in older patients?
options: A. Right upper quadrant
B. Right lower quadrant
C. Left lower quadrant
D. Epigastric
questions.rationale: Diverticulitis classically presents with left lower quadrant pain, especially in older adults.

questions.question: A middle-aged man with cardiac risk factors reports epigastric discomfort and nausea, without classic chest pain. What should be considered?
options: A. Simple indigestion only, no further workup needed
B. Inferior MI presenting atypically as epigastric pain
C. This can only be a GI complaint
D. Anxiety, with no cardiac workup needed
questions.rationale: Inferior MI can present with epigastric discomfort and nausea rather than classic chest pain, so an ECG should be obtained in patients with cardiac risk factors presenting this way.

questions.question: Why can lower lobe pneumonia cause upper abdominal pain?
options: A. It cannot — pneumonia never causes abdominal pain
B. Diaphragmatic irritation from the adjacent lung inflammation can refer pain to the upper abdomen
C. Pneumonia always presents exclusively with chest pain
D. This is caused by direct bacterial spread to the abdomen
questions.rationale: Diaphragmatic irritation from lower lobe pneumonia can refer pain to the upper abdomen, making a respiratory assessment important in some abdominal-pain presentations.

questions.question: Which endocrine condition commonly presents with abdominal pain as a symptom, alongside Kussmaul respirations?
options: A. Hypoglycemia
B. HHNS
C. Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
D. Hypothyroidism
questions.rationale: Abdominal pain is a recognized DKA symptom, often accompanying Kussmaul respirations, polyuria, and polydipsia.

questions.question: Why is using abdominal pain location as an organizing framework clinically useful?
options: A. It has no real diagnostic value
B. It provides strong differential clues (RLQ, RUQ, LLQ, epigastric) that speed formation of a useful differential diagnosis
C. Location never correlates with the underlying cause
D. It replaces the need for vital signs
questions.rationale: While not absolute, pain location (RLQ→appendicitis, RUQ→cholecystitis, LLQ→diverticulitis, epigastric-to-back→pancreatitis) is a strong organizing tool for building a differential quickly.

questions.question: What cardiac risk does hyperkalemia in a missed-dialysis patient carry?
options: A. No cardiac risk at all
B. Dangerous cardiac dysrhythmias
C. Only mild palpitations with no clinical significance
D. Guaranteed cardiac arrest in all cases
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia is associated with dangerous cardiac dysrhythmias, making cardiac monitoring and urgent evaluation important in at-risk dialysis patients.

questions.question: A patient with vomiting and diarrhea over two days has dry mucous membranes and tenting skin, but normal blood pressure and heart rate. What is the correct interpretation?
options: A. No dehydration is present since vital signs are normal
B. Early/mild dehydration is likely present based on exam findings, even before vital sign changes appear
C. This patient requires no further monitoring
D. These findings are unrelated to fluid status
questions.rationale: Skin turgor and mucous membrane findings can reveal dehydration before compensatory vital sign changes (tachycardia, hypotension) appear — similar to how compensated shock can precede a falling blood pressure.

questions.question: Which practice best protects against missing a non-abdominal cause of an 'abdominal pain' complaint?
options: A. Assuming all abdominal pain is gastrointestinal in origin
B. Systematic assessment including vital signs, glucose check, and ECG when cardiac risk factors are present
C. Skipping vital signs to save time
D. Treating every abdominal complaint identically regardless of history
questions.rationale: A systematic assessment — not anchoring on 'GI' — catches referred/mimicking causes like inferior MI, DKA, or pneumonia presenting as abdominal pain.
